content.body: The circulatory system transports blood around the body, delivering oxygen and nutrients to cells and removing carbon dioxide and waste products. It consists of the heart, blood vessels, and blood.

content.title: The Heart
content.body:
The heart is a muscular pump divided into 4 chambers:
Right atrium — receives deoxygenated blood from the body
Right ventricle — pumps deoxygenated blood to the lungs
Left atrium — receives oxygenated blood from the lungs
Left ventricle — pumps oxygenated blood to the body (thickest wall)

Valves prevent backflow of blood between chambers.

Double circulation in humans:
Pulmonary circulation: Right heart → Lungs → Left heart
Systemic circulation: Left heart → Body → Right heart

This keeps oxygenated and deoxygenated blood separate.

content.title: Blood Vessels
content.body:
Arteries — carry blood AWAY from the heart
Thick muscular walls, small lumen, high pressure
Carry oxygenated blood (except pulmonary artery)

Veins — carry blood TOWARDS the heart
Thinner walls, large lumen, low pressure
Have valves to prevent backflow
Carry deoxygenated blood (except pulmonary vein)

Capillaries — very thin walls (one cell thick)
Connect arteries to veins
Where exchange of gases and nutrients occurs

The heartbeat is controlled by electrical signals from a natural pacemaker called the sinoatrial (SA) node in the right atrium. A normal resting heart rate is 60–100 beats per minute. Exercise increases heart rate to supply more oxygen to working muscles.

questions.question: How many chambers does the human heart have?
questions.explanation: The human heart has 4 chambers: right atrium, right ventricle, left atrium, and left ventricle.

questions.question: Which chamber has the thickest wall and why?
options: Left ventricle — pumps blood to the whole body
questions.answer: Left ventricle — pumps blood to the whole body
questions.explanation: The left ventricle has the thickest muscular wall because it must pump blood at high pressure to reach all parts of the body.

questions.question: What do arteries do?
options: Carry blood away from the heart
questions.answer: Carry blood away from the heart
questions.explanation: Arteries carry blood away from the heart. They have thick muscular walls to withstand high blood pressure.

questions.question: Why do veins have valves?
questions.explanation: Valves in veins ensure blood flows in one direction — towards the heart. Without them, gravity would cause blood to flow backwards.

questions.question: Where does gas exchange between blood and cells occur?
questions.explanation: Capillaries have walls only one cell thick, allowing oxygen and nutrients to pass into cells and carbon dioxide and waste to pass out.

questions.question: What is pulmonary circulation?
options: Blood flow from heart to body and back
Blood flow between heart and lungs
questions.answer: Blood flow between heart and lungs
questions.explanation: Pulmonary circulation is the pathway from the right heart to the lungs (to pick up oxygen) and back to the left heart.

questions.question: Which blood vessel carries deoxygenated blood from the heart to the lungs?
questions.explanation: The pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle to the lungs. Note: it is the exception — most arteries carry oxygenated blood.

questions.question: Why does exercise increase heart rate?
options: Muscles need more oxygen and glucose
questions.answer: Muscles need more oxygen and glucose
questions.explanation: During exercise, muscles work harder and need more oxygen and glucose for respiration. The heart beats faster to deliver more blood.

questions.question: What is the normal resting heart rate?
questions.explanation: A normal resting heart rate for adults is 60-100 beats per minute. Athletes often have lower resting heart rates.

questions.question: What controls the natural heartbeat?
options: SA node (pacemaker) in the right atrium
questions.answer: SA node (pacemaker) in the right atrium
questions.explanation: The sinoatrial (SA) node is the natural pacemaker of the heart. It generates electrical impulses that trigger each heartbeat.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

content.body: Blood is a connective tissue that transports substances around the body. An adult human body contains about 5 litres of blood. Blood is made up of plasma, red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets.

content.title: Plasma
content.body:
Plasma is the yellow liquid part of blood — about 55% of blood volume.
It is 90% water and carries:
• Dissolved nutrients (glucose, amino acids, vitamins)
• Carbon dioxide (as bicarbonate ions)
• Hormones (chemical messengers)
• Waste products (urea)
• Plasma proteins (fibrinogen for clotting)

content.title: Red Blood Cells (Erythrocytes)
content.body:
Most numerous cells in blood
Biconcave shape — increases surface area for gas exchange
No nucleus — more room for haemoglobin
Contain haemoglobin — binds to oxygen to form oxyhaemoglobin
Live for about 120 days
Made in red bone marrow
Destroyed in the spleen

content.title: White Blood Cells (Leukocytes)
content.body:
Part of the immune system — defend against disease
Two main types:
1. Phagocytes — engulf and destroy bacteria (phagocytosis)
2. Lymphocytes — produce antibodies that neutralise pathogens

Fewer in number than red blood cells
Have a nucleus
Live for days to years

content.title: Platelets (Thrombocytes)
content.body:
Tiny cell fragments (no nucleus)
Role: blood clotting
When a blood vessel is damaged:
1. Platelets rush to the wound and clump together
2. Clotting factors convert fibrinogen → fibrin
3. Fibrin forms a mesh that traps red blood cells
4. A clot (scab) forms, stopping bleeding

questions.question: What percentage of blood is plasma?
questions.explanation: Plasma makes up about 55% of blood volume. It is the liquid portion that carries dissolved substances.

questions.question: What is the role of haemoglobin in red blood cells?
questions.explanation: Haemoglobin is a red protein that binds to oxygen in the lungs to form oxyhaemoglobin, and releases it to tissues.

questions.question: Why do red blood cells have no nucleus?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells lose their nucleus during development, creating more space for haemoglobin to carry more oxygen.

questions.question: What do phagocytes do?
questions.explanation: Phagocytes are white blood cells that engulf and digest bacteria and other foreign particles through phagocytosis.

questions.question: What do lymphocytes produce?
questions.explanation: Lymphocytes are white blood cells that produce antibodies — proteins that specifically target and neutralise pathogens.

questions.question: What is the role of platelets?
questions.explanation: Platelets are cell fragments that clump at wound sites and trigger the clotting cascade to stop bleeding.

questions.question: Where are red blood cells made?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells are produced in the red bone marrow (found in flat bones like the sternum, ribs, and pelvis).

questions.question: Carbon dioxide is mainly transported in blood as...
questions.explanation: About 70% of CO₂ is transported as bicarbonate ions (HCO₃⁻) dissolved in plasma. A small amount is carried by haemoglobin.

questions.question: How long do red blood cells typically live?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells live for approximately 120 days before being broken down and recycled, mainly in the spleen.

questions.question: Which protein forms a mesh during blood clotting?
questions.explanation: Fibrinogen (a plasma protein) is converted to fibrin during clotting. Fibrin forms a mesh that traps red blood cells and forms a clot.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

lessons.title: Transport in Plants — Xylem and Phloem

content.body: Plants also have a transport system to move water, minerals, and food around the plant. Unlike animals, plants do not have a pump (heart). Instead, they use two types of vessels: xylem and phloem.

content.title: Xylem Vessels
content.body:
Transport: Water and dissolved mineral salts
Direction: UPWARD only — from roots to leaves
Process: Transpiration pull (water evaporates from leaves creating a suction)
Structure: Dead hollow tubes with lignin-strengthened walls
Found in: roots, stems, leaves

Remember: Xylem = eXit water upward

content.title: Phloem Tubes
content.body:
Transport: Dissolved sugars (mainly sucrose) made in leaves
Direction: UP and DOWN — from leaves to all parts of plant
Process: Translocation
Structure: Living cells with sieve plates
Found in: roots, stems, leaves

Remember: Phloem = Food (moves Food around)

Transpiration:
Water absorbed by roots → travels up xylem → evaporates from leaves through stomata

Factors that increase transpiration rate:
• Higher temperature
• Lower humidity
• Increased wind speed
• More light (stomata open wider)

content.title: Root Hair Cells and Water Absorption
content.body:
Root hair cells have long extensions that greatly increase surface area
Water enters by OSMOSIS — moving from high water concentration in soil to lower water concentration inside the root
Mineral salts enter by ACTIVE TRANSPORT — requiring energy
This is why waterlogged (oxygen-free) soil harms plants — roots need oxygen for active transport

questions.question: What does xylem transport?
questions.explanation: Xylem transports water and dissolved mineral salts from the roots upward to the leaves.

questions.question: What does phloem transport?
questions.explanation: Phloem transports dissolved sugars (mainly sucrose) produced in the leaves to all parts of the plant — both up and down.

questions.question: What is the process of water evaporating from leaves called?
questions.explanation: Transpiration is the evaporation of water from the leaves through tiny pores called stomata.

questions.question: Which factor would INCREASE the rate of transpiration?
questions.explanation: Wind removes water vapour from around the stomata, maintaining a high concentration gradient, so water evaporates faster.

questions.question: Water enters root hair cells by which process?
questions.explanation: Water moves from the higher water concentration in the soil to the lower water concentration inside root hair cells by osmosis.

questions.question: Why are xylem vessels made of dead cells?
options: Dead cells form hollow continuous tubes for water flow
questions.answer: Dead cells form hollow continuous tubes for water flow
questions.explanation: Dead xylem cells form hollow, continuous tubes with no obstructions (no organelles or cytoplasm), allowing water to flow freely.

questions.question: What is translocation?
questions.explanation: Translocation is the transport of dissolved organic substances (mainly sucrose) through the phloem to all parts of the plant.

questions.question: Why does waterlogged soil harm plants?
options: No oxygen for active transport of minerals
questions.answer: No oxygen for active transport of minerals
questions.explanation: Roots need oxygen for active transport (energy-requiring) to absorb mineral salts. Waterlogged soil has no air, so roots cannot absorb minerals.

questions.question: Through which structures does water evaporate from leaves?
questions.explanation: Stomata are tiny pores on the surface of leaves. They open to allow gas exchange and water vapour evaporates through them.

questions.question: What creates the 'pull' that draws water up the xylem?
questions.explanation: As water evaporates from leaves, it creates a tension (suction) that pulls water up through the xylem — this is called the transpiration pull.

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

lessons.title: Transport in Living Organisms — Advanced Part 4

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Transport in Living Organisms. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Transport in Living Organisms concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Transport in Living Organisms builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 4.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Transport in Living Organisms. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 4.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Transport in Living Organisms. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Transport in Living Organisms include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Transport in Living Organisms. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).

lessons.title: Transport in Living Organisms — Advanced Part 6

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Transport in Living Organisms. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Transport in Living Organisms concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of biology.
Core Theory

This section of Transport in Living Organisms builds on foundational knowledge. In biology, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 6.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Transport in Living Organisms. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 6.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Transport in Living Organisms. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Transport in Living Organisms include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Transport in Living Organisms. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the basic unit of life?
questions.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

questions.question: Which organelle is responsible for energy production in cells?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration to produce ATP — the cell's energy currency. Hence called the 'powerhouse of the cell'.

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
The breakdown of glucose for energy
The absorption of minerals from soil
questions.answer: The process by which plants make food using sunlight
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂. Plants convert light energy to chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.question: What is osmosis?
options: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.answer: Movement of water from high to low water potential through a semi-permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the passive movement of water molecules through a semi-permeable membrane from a region of higher water potential to lower.

questions.question: What molecule carries genetic information?
questions.explanation: DNA carries the genetic code in sequences of bases (A, T, G, C) that determine the structure of proteins.

questions.question: What is the function of chloroplasts?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, capturing light energy to make glucose.

questions.question: In genetics, what is a dominant allele?
options: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
One that is only expressed when two copies are present
questions.answer: One that is expressed even when only one copy is present
questions.explanation: A dominant allele is expressed in the phenotype regardless of whether it is heterozygous or homozygous.

questions.question: What is diffusion?
options: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.answer: Movement of particles from high to low concentration without energy
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the passive movement of particles down a concentration gradient — no energy (ATP) is required.

questions.question: Which blood component carries oxygen?
questions.explanation: Red blood cells contain haemoglobin, which binds to oxygen in the lungs and releases it in body tissues.

questions.question: What is the role of the nucleus?
options: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.answer: Controls cell activities and contains DNA
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the control centre of the cell, containing DNA and directing all cellular activities including protein synthesis.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.answer: The process of releasing energy from glucose in cells
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP. It releases energy from glucose for cell use.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
All organisms evolving at the same rate
questions.answer: Organisms with favourable traits survive and reproduce more
questions.explanation: Natural selection is Darwin's mechanism: individuals with traits better suited to the environment survive and pass on those traits.

questions.question: What organ filters blood in humans?
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products (urea, excess water, salts), producing urine.

questions.question: What is the function of stomata in leaves?
questions.explanation: Stomata are pores in leaves that allow CO₂ in and O₂ out during photosynthesis, and water vapour out during transpiration.

questions.question: Which part of the neurone carries impulses away from the cell body?
questions.explanation: The axon is the long fibre that carries nerve impulses away from the cell body to other neurons or effectors.

questions.question: What is the function of enzymes?
options: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.answer: Biological catalysts that speed up reactions
questions.explanation: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower activation energy and speed up biochemical reactions without being consumed.

questions.question: What gas do plants absorb during photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ from the air through stomata during photosynthesis, converting it to glucose using light energy.

questions.question: What is a food chain?
options: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
A list of foods animals eat
questions.answer: A sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to another
questions.explanation: A food chain shows the transfer of energy from producers (plants) through consumers. Example: Grass → Rabbit → Fox.

questions.question: What is the function of the cell membrane?
options: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.answer: Controls what enters and leaves the cell (selectively permeable)
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the passage of substances in and out of the cell.

questions.question: What is mitosis?
options: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.answer: Cell division producing two genetically identical daughter cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis is cell division for growth and repair, producing two daughter cells with identical chromosomes to the parent cell (diploid).
